message.content:
Google Search Central and web.dev publish these as the main **mobile / page-experience checks** you can test directly on a page or site. Below is an exhaustive, testable checklist for the signals you named, with the exact Google documentation URLs.

| Check | What it verifies | Exact source URL |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile-first indexing parity** | The mobile version contains the same primary content, headings, structured data, metadata, and equivalent URLs/annotations as the desktop version, so Google can index the same information from mobile that it sees on desktop.[3] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing` |
| **Responsive design / viewport** | The page is configured for responsive rendering on mobile using a proper viewport setup, so Google can understand the page as mobile-friendly and content is not forced into a desktop layout on small screens.[5] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide` |
| **Tap target sizing** | Links and buttons are large enough and spaced so users can tap them easily on mobile without accidental taps; this is a mobile usability requirement tied to page experience.[5] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide` |
| **Legible font sizes** | Text is readable on a mobile screen without zooming, which is part of Google’s mobile-friendly guidance.[5] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide` |
| **No intrusive interstitials** | The page does not use intrusive interstitials or large overlays that obstruct content on mobile; Google explicitly warns these can hurt mobile search visibility and user experience.[3] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing` |
| **HTTPS** | The page is served over a secure connection, which is a foundational Google SEO / page-quality requirement.[5] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide` |
| **Core Web Vitals: LCP** | The page meets Google’s largest-contentful-paint guidance for loading performance. The current Core Web Vitals threshold is **good at \( \le 2.5 \) seconds**.[6] | `https://web.dev/articles/lcp` |
| **Core Web Vitals: INP** | The page meets Google’s interaction responsiveness guidance. The current Core Web Vitals threshold is **good at \( \le 200 \) milliseconds**.[7] | `https://web.dev/articles/inp` |
| **Core Web Vitals: CLS** | The page has low visual instability during load and interaction. The current Core Web Vitals threshold is **good at \( \le 0.1 \)**.[8] | `https://web.dev/articles/cls` |
| **No content wider than screen** | The page avoids horizontal overflow and content that extends beyond the viewport, which is part of Google’s mobile-friendly guidance.[5] | `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide` |

A few important notes for test design:

- For **mobile-first indexing parity**, Google’s guidance is not just “same text”; it explicitly calls for the mobile site to match desktop content quality, headings, structured data, metadata, image alt text, and correct canonical/alternate handling for separate URLs.[3]
- For **Core Web Vitals**, use the current web.dev metric pages as the canonical threshold references: LCP \(\le 2.5s\), INP \(\le 200ms\), CLS \(\le 0.1\).[6][7][8]
- For **responsive/mobile usability checks** like viewport, tap targets, legible text, and no horizontal scrolling, Google’s SEO Starter Guide is the relevant Search Central source in the material provided here.[5]

If you want, I can turn this into a **QA test matrix** with columns for *test step, pass/fail criteria, tool, and evidence to capture*.
